retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Visual Cortex -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The visual cortex is the primary cortical region of the brain that receives, integrates, and processes visual information relayed from the retinas. It is in the occipital lobe of the primary cerebral cortex, which is in the most posterior region of the brain. The visual cortex divides into five different areas (V1 to V5) based on function and structure. Visual information from the retinas that are traveling to the visual cortex first passes through the thalamus, where it synapses in a nucleus called the lateral geniculate. This information then leaves the lateral geniculate and travels to V1, the first area of the visual cortex. V1 is also known as the primary visual cortex and centers around the calcarine sulcus. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Visual Cortex -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: V1 is the first of the cortical regions to receive and process information and also the best-understood portion of the visual cortex. V1 is divided up into six distinct layers, each comprising different cell types and functions. Notably, layer 4 is the location that receives information from the lateral geniculate. Layer 4 is also the layer that has the highest concentration of simple cells. Complex cells, on the other hand, can be found in layers 2, 3, and 6. V1 responds to simple visual components such as orientation and direction. The summation of this information provides the foundation for more complicated pattern recognition later in the visual stream. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Visual Cortex -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The primary function of the visual cortex is to process visual information. The visual cortex subdivides into five different areas based on structural and functional classifications. The hypothesis is that as visual information gets passed along, each subsequent cortical area is more specialized than the last. Neurons in the visual cortex often respond to stimuli within a fixed receptive field, the area of the visual field that they respond to, and the neurons in each visual area respond to different types of stimuli. One of the best-studied examples of specialized cells is that of simple and complex cells. Simple cells, which are found mostly in V1, respond to specific types of visual cues, such as the orientation of edges and lines. Complex cells, which occur in V1-V3, are like simple cells in that they respond to edges and orientations, but they do not appear to represent a single receptive field. Instead, they respond to the summation of several receptive fields that become integrated from many simple cells. Also, complex cells respond preferentially to movement in specific directions. Other examples of specialized cells include end-stopped cells, which detect line endings, and bar and grating cells. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Visual Cortex -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Each hemisphere has its own visual cortex, which receives information from the contralateral visual field. In other words, the right cortical areas process information from the left eye, and the left processes information from the right eye. The primary purpose of the visual cortex is to receive, segment, and integrate visual information. The processed information from the visual cortex is subsequently sent to other regions of the brain to be analyzed and utilized. This process is highly specialized and allows the brain to recognize objects and patterns quickly without a significant conscious effort.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Occipital Lobe -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The occipital lobe is the visual processing area of the brain. It is associated with visuospatial processing, distance and depth perception, color determination, object and face recognition, and memory formation. The primary visual cortex, also known as V1 or Brodmann area 17, surrounds the calcarine sulcus on the occipital lobe's medial aspect. It receives the visual information from the retina via the thalamus. The secondary visual cortex, also known as V2, V3, V4, V5, or Brodmann areas 18 and 19, surrounds the primary cortex and receives information from it.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Visual Cortex -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Visual Cortex -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: As visual information disseminates throughout the brain, more specialized cells are present. The theory is that there are specialized cells or groups of cells that learn to respond to particular objects; this would allow for the immediate recognition of things seen previously. [6] Also, similar cells could be responsible for other important visual information, such as spatial orientation.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Visual Cortex -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: V2 receives integrated information from V1 and subsequently has an increased level of complexity and response patterns to objects. Researchers have recorded cells in this region responding to differences in color, spatial frequency, moderately complex patterns, and object orientation. [5] V2 sends feedback connections to V1 and has feedforward connections with V3-V5. Information leaving the second visual area splits into the dorsal and ventral streams, which specialize in processing different aspects of visual information. The former is often described as being concerned with object recognition, while the latter focuses on spatial tasks and visual-motor skills.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cerebral Cortex Functions -- Function -- The Occipital Lobe
retrieved_docs.content: The occipital lobe is the smallest lobe in the cerebrum cortex. It is located in the most posterior region of the brain, posterior to the parietal lobe and temporal lobe. The role of this lobe is visual processing and interpretation. Typically based on the function and structure, the visual cortex is divided into five areas (v1-v5). The primary visual cortex (v1, BA 17) is the first area that receives the visual information from the thalamus, and its located around the calcarine sulcus. The visual cortex receive, process, interpret the visual information, then this processed information is sent to the other regions of the brain to be further analyzed (example: inferior temporal lobe). This visual information helps us to determine, recognize, and compare the objects to each other. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Visual Cortex -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: One advantage of this specialization is that other cortical regions are free to perform other computations, such as those responsible for executive functioning and decision-making. However, since visual processing is mostly an unconscious process, it can lead to misinterpretations of visual data, which are demonstrated by the efficacy of visual illusions. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Visual Cortex -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Many surgical cases show the risk of damage to the visual cortex. [8] The visual cortex is susceptible to mechanical damage that can be caused by surgeons attempting to remove tumors or other masses that are present in this area. To minimize the possibility of causing cortical blindness, surgeons will often use a technique called cortical mapping to try and localize important regions of visual processing.

retrieved_docs.title: Visual Discrimination -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Visual discrimination is the ability to detect differences and classify objects, symbols, or shapes based on characteristics such as color, position, form, pattern, texture, and size. In the simplest terms, the eyes receive input from the environment, and various cells detect and transmit signals to the brain. Specifically, the visual cortex, located in the occipital lobe, is responsible for processing the shape and orientation of objects. The 3 cell types in the primary visual cortex—simple, complex, and hypercomplex—demonstrate correspondingly increased ability to respond to motion and degree of linearity. A more detailed explanation of the visual cortex is covered in a separate neuroanatomy topic. Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Neuroanatomy, Visual Cortex ," for more information.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Visual Cortex -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Cortical mapping allows surgeons to localize specific regions of the visual cortex by stimulating the brain parenchyma with an electrode and asking the patient to report what they saw. This patient feedback is crucial when a differential diagnosis of malignancy from normal brain tissue is difficult. With this and other similar techniques, surgeons can make informed decisions on where to cut and what structures they can safely remove.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Visual Cortex -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Cortical blindness can result from any disruption or damage to the normal architecture of the visual cortex. Most commonly, this occurs in patients with a stroke in the territory of the posterior cerebral artery. [7] If an ischemic stroke causes cortical blindness, there is the possibility that visual impairment is reversible through reperfusion. However, many patients do not fully regain cortical function and maintain some level of cortical visual impairment. Prosopagnosia is a term referring to the inability to recognize faces and can result from a stroke that inhibits information traveling from the visual cortex to the inferior temporal cortex. Other non-stroke causes of cortical blindness include infection, eclampsia, traumatic brain injury, encephalitis, meningitis, medications, and hyperammonemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Occipital Lobe -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The primary visual cortex transmits information through two pathways: the dorsal and ventral stream. The dorsal stream is associated with object location and carries visual information to the parietal lobe. The ventral stream has associations with object recognition and transmits visual information to the temporal lobe.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cerebral Cortex -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The cerebral cortex contains sensory, motor and important association areas. The thalamus receives somatosensory information and conveys it to the primary somatosensory cortex in the postcentral gyrus of the parietal lobe. Other important primary cortical sensory areas include the temporal lobe auditory cortex and the occipital lobe visual cortex. Each sensory area has associated sensations given specific stimuli, providing meaning to sensations. The motor regions of the cerebral cortex are located predominantly in the frontal lobe, anterior to the central sulcus, and include the primary motor cortex (found in the precentral gyrus) and the premotor cortex, which initiates and regulates voluntary movement.

Together, these entities and relationships highlight the importance of precise anatomic localization to the eye and its lens capsule for accurate diagnosis, surgical planning, and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1968
retrieved_docs.content: Operative procedure on anterior segment of eye is directly related to the eye (oči), indicating that surgical interventions targeting the front structures of the eye are procedures performed on the ocular organ.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 49

Ocular diagnostic procedures are organized under uncategorized histopathology procedures, a parent category that also includes anatomy, indicating that eye histopathology forms part of a broader anatomical and pathological assessment framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: detachment of the eye (انفصال مشيمية العين) and hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي). Peripheral involvement includes peripheral disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis and peripheral focal retinitis with retinochoroiditis. Across all entities, the choroidea (choroid) is the anatomical locus of the characteristic lesions, underscoring its central role in pathophysiology, clinical assessment, precise diagnosis, and tailored management of choroidal and retinal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 823
retrieved_docs.content: Intracapsular extraction of lens is classified as a type of lens and cataract procedure, indicating that it belongs to the broader group of surgical interventions aimed at treating lens pathology and cataract formation. This procedure can be organized according to the “Operations by approach” framework, reflecting that the surgical technique is defined by the anatomical route used to access the lens. A more detailed variant, the one‑stage intracapsular extraction of lens by inferior temporal route and insertion of a prosthetic intraocular lens, is also identified as a lens and cataract procedure. This specific operation combines removal of the lens through an inferior temporal approach with immediate implantation of an artificial intraocular lens, and it is performed within the eye (procedure site of the eye). Together, these relationships illustrate how intracapsular lens extraction procedures are categorized, differentiated by surgical approach, and linked to their anatomical site of execution, providing a clear hierarchy and contextual understanding for clinicians planning cataract surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2448
retrieved_docs.content: The structure of intracranial artery serves as the anatomical site where the pathological finding termed تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial arterial sclerosis) is identified, indicating that this form of arterial sclerosis occurs within the brain’s arterial architecture and is relevant for diagnostic and therapeutic considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 189
retrieved_docs.content: links imaging findings, anatomical structures, injury classifications, and interpretation tools, providing a comprehensive view of neurological injuries, vascular and inflammatory disorders, and related movement disorders essential for accurate diagnosis, coding, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2372
retrieved_docs.content: One stage intracapsular extraction of lens by inferior temporal route and insertion of prosthetic intraocular lens is an ocular surgical technique that is designated as a procedure_site_of the eye. The operative procedure on anterior segment of eye has relationship to the eye (oči). Both interventions involve direct manipulation of ocular structures, underscoring that the eye serves as the anatomical location for these procedures.

Precise identification of choroidal involvement at this site is essential for accurate diagnosis, management, and prognostication of the diverse ocular diseases that arise within the choroidea.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2029
retrieved_docs.content: One stage intracapsular extraction of the lens by inferior temporal route and insertion of prosthetic intraocular lens is a surgical procedure that is performed on the eye.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1964
retrieved_docs.content: An abnormally opaque structure, identified as a morphologic abnormality, is directly addressed through the extraction of a primary membranous cataract by excision, representing a direct morphological intervention aimed at removing the opaque lesion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2021
retrieved_docs.content: The category “إجراءات هيستوباثولوجيا، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” (histopathology procedures not classified elsewhere) functions as a broad parent classification for specialized medical procedures. Within this hierarchy, “diagnostic procedures on eye” are identified as a child of this parent, indicating that eye‑focused diagnostic methods are grouped under the umbrella of unclassified histopathology procedures. Likewise, “التشريح” (anatomical or dissection procedures) is also listed as a child of the same parent category, showing that both eye diagnostic procedures and anatomical/dissection procedures share the same higher‑level classification. This structured relationship clarifies how specific diagnostic and anatomical interventions are organized within broader histopathology categories, supporting accurate documentation, coding, and coordinated patient care.

Additionally, the disease referred to in Arabic as “داء ريفسام” manifests its clinical findings in the retina, reinforcing that retinal involvement—whether from systemic metabolic disorders, primary retinal diseases, or targeted retinal lesion destruction—requires precise identification of the retinal site for accurate diagnosis and treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2128
retrieved_docs.content: taxonomy aids clinicians in recognizing the diverse presentations of choroidal and retinal pathology, facilitating accurate diagnosis, targeted management, and coordinated patient care across a range of ocular disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: hierarchically. “Destruction of localized lesion of retina (eg, macular edema, tumors), one or more sessions; radiation by implantation of source” is a child procedure of the broader category “Destructive procedure on posterior segment of eye,” which itself is a parent of “destruction of lesion of retina and choroid.” The procedure “destruction of lesion of retina and choroid” has a direct procedure site of the retina, confirming that these destructive ocular treatments are directed at the same anatomical location affected by the diabetic retinopathies. In summary, secondary diabetes mellitus leads to retinal and renal complications, with the retina serving as the primary site of disease manifestation and therapeutic targeting. Understanding the hierarchical classification of secondary diabetes mellitus, its specific complications (various forms of retinopathy and nephropathy), and the relationship between these complications and ocular destructive procedures is essential for
